behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Requires validating an external error-path HTTP contract (401) and that no enqueue side effect occurs after a real request/body parse path. Unit tests of HMAC logic can pass while real HTTP/raw-body wiring fails.
behaviors.test_description: Send a real HTTP webhook with an intentionally invalid `X-Signature-256` over the running service; assert 401 response and verify via queue readback/inspection that no event was persisted.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: Explicit round-trip side-effect ordering across an external boundary: persistence must be observable before ack. This is a SYSTEM TRIAD #3 case (prove externally visible side effect via readback).
behaviors.test_description: Issue a real HTTP webhook, capture ack timing, and in the same test confirm the event is durably present in the real persistent queue before considering the 200 check satisfied.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Cross-component orchestration check: "returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing" verifies handoff/decoupling between handler and async worker. No explicit requirement for real network/subprocess or external API contract beyond local orchestration.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler + queue + downstream worker modules in-process; make downstream processing intentionally slow and assert response completes before worker completion while enqueue still occurs.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System because behavior text does not require real external infra semantics; it focuses on internal async decoupling.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Wall-clock latency under concurrency is runtime behavior that commonly diverges from unit/in-process assumptions; must be validated through real HTTP serving path and scheduler/event-loop contention.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service, preload slow concurrent downstream work, then send real HTTP requests and assert end-to-end response latency stays under 3 seconds.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: State propagation/idempotency across modules: "ID was already received" -> dedupe state must prevent second write in queue path. This is module handoff/shared-state behavior, not model output or required real external infra.
behaviors.test_description: With real dedupe + queue modules wired together (local persistence allowed), submit same event ID twice and assert only one persisted queue record exists.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System because behavior text does not explicitly require real external HTTP/API contract verification; core risk is cross-module state handling.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Tests translation of queue-state signal to HTTP response contract across components: "queue reports it is full" -> handler maps to 503 + `Retry-After`. Main failure mode is inter-module error mapping/propagation.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler to queue module, force/trigger queue-full condition, and assert HTTP 503 plus correct `Retry-After` header are produced.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System because the behavior does not require proving real external queue provider contracts; it can be validated with integrated local components and deterministic full-state trigger.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Queue-component + persistence interaction: "persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity" is beyond unit logic and needs real queue module with its persistence backend (local DB/filesystem acceptable). No LLM or multi-agent steps.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate queue with real persistence backend and a small capacity, push events until limit, then assert next write is rejected and persisted count never exceeds configured max.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System because behavior text does not explicitly require real network/subprocess/external API boundary; local persistent backend integration is sufficient to expose the target failure.
